chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Nature of Matter: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures

flashcards.q: What is a mixture?
flashcards.a: A mixture is formed when two or more substances are mixed together while each substance retains its own properties and does not react chemically with other components.

flashcards.q: Define a pure substance.
flashcards.a: A pure substance is matter that has no other substance present in it and consists entirely of one type of particle. It cannot be separated into other kinds of matter by any physical process.

flashcards.q: What is a uniform mixture?
flashcards.a: A uniform mixture is one where components are evenly distributed and cannot be seen separately, even under a microscope. All parts appear the same throughout.

flashcards.q: What is a non-uniform mixture?
flashcards.a: A non-uniform mixture is one where different components are visible with the naked eye or with a magnifying device. The components appear as separate, distinguishable parts.

flashcards.q: Give two examples of uniform mixtures.
flashcards.a: Examples: Air (nitrogen, oxygen, argon, carbon dioxide mixed uniformly), sugar dissolved in water, salt dissolved in water, vinegar (acetic acid in water).

flashcards.q: Give two examples of non-uniform mixtures.
flashcards.a: Examples: Sprout salad (with visible green gram, chickpeas, onion, tomato), sand and pebbles mixed, oil and water, baking powder (baking soda and tartaric acid visible as particles).

flashcards.q: What is an alloy?
flashcards.a: An alloy is a uniform mixture of two or more metals mixed so thoroughly that the entire mixture appears the same throughout, and individual substances cannot be seen separately.

flashcards.q: Name three alloys and their composition.
flashcards.a: Stainless steel (iron + nickel + chromium + carbon), Brass (copper + zinc), Bronze (copper + tin).

flashcards.q: Why are alloys useful?
flashcards.a: Alloys possess superior strength, durability, and corrosion resistance compared to pure metals. Their properties are tailored by combining metals in specific ratios.

flashcards.q: Is air a mixture or a pure substance?
flashcards.a: Air is a mixture. Specifically, it is a uniform mixture of nitrogen (~78%), oxygen (~21%), argon, carbon dioxide, and water vapour.

flashcards.q: What is an element?
flashcards.a: An element is a pure substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by any chemical or physical process. It is made up of identical atoms and is the building block of all matter.

flashcards.q: What is an atom?
flashcards.a: An atom is the smallest particle of an element, made up of identical particles that are different from the atoms of any other element.

flashcards.q: What is a molecule?
flashcards.a: A molecule is a stable particle formed when two or more atoms of the same element or different elements combine, since most atoms cannot exist independently.

flashcards.q: Give examples of molecules formed from single elements.
flashcards.a: Hydrogen molecule (H₂)—two hydrogen atoms, Oxygen molecule (O₂)—two oxygen atoms, Nitrogen molecule (N₂)—two nitrogen atoms.

flashcards.q: What is a compound?
flashcards.a: A compound is a pure substance formed when two or more different elements combine chemically in a fixed ratio to create a completely new substance with properties entirely different from its constituent elements.

flashcards.q: How does water demonstrate compound properties?
flashcards.a: Water (H₂O) is formed from hydrogen (flammable gas) and oxygen (supports combustion), yet water is a colorless liquid that extinguishes fire, showing completely different properties from its constituent elements.

flashcards.q: How does sodium chloride demonstrate compound properties?
flashcards.a: Sodium chloride (NaCl) is formed from sodium (soft, reactive metal) and chlorine (toxic, hazardous gas), yet salt is harmless, non-reactive, and essential for life—showing completely different properties.

flashcards.q: What is the chemical formula of water?
flashcards.a: H₂O—water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms bonded to 1 oxygen atom in a fixed 2:1 ratio.

flashcards.q: What is the chemical formula of common salt?
flashcards.a: NaCl—sodium chloride consists of 1 sodium atom bonded to 1 chlorine atom in a fixed 1:1 ratio.

flashcards.q: What is the chemical formula of sugar?
flashcards.a: C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁—glucose/sugar consists of 12 carbon atoms, 22 hydrogen atoms, and 11 oxygen atoms in a fixed ratio.

flashcards.q: Classify the following as metal or non-metal: Gold, Oxygen, Iron, Carbon
flashcards.a: Metals: Gold, Iron. Non-metals: Oxygen, Carbon.

flashcards.q: What are metalloids?
flashcards.a: Metalloids are elements that have intermediate properties between metals and non-metals. Examples: Silicon, Boron.

flashcards.q: How many elements are known today?
flashcards.a: 118 elements are known today. Most exist as solids at room temperature, 11 exist as gases, and only 2 (mercury and bromine) exist as liquids.

flashcards.q: Name two elements that exist as gases at room temperature.
flashcards.a: Oxygen (O₂), Nitrogen (N₂), Hydrogen (H₂), Helium (He), Chlorine (Cl₂).

flashcards.q: Name the only two elements that are liquids at room temperature.
flashcards.a: Mercury (Hg)—a metal, and Bromine (Br)—a non-metal.

flashcards.q: What is adulteration?
flashcards.a: Adulteration is an illegal process of adding cheaper or poor-quality substances to a product to increase quantity or reduce manufacturing costs, deteriorating product quality and making it hazardous to health.

flashcards.q: How is 'pure' in common usage different from 'pure' in science?
flashcards.a: In common usage, 'pure' means unadulterated (no harmful additions). In science, 'pure' means the substance consists entirely of one type of particle—no mixtures of different substances.

flashcards.q: How can water be decomposed into its elements?
flashcards.a: Water can be decomposed through electrolysis: passing electric current through water breaks it down into hydrogen gas (H₂) and oxygen gas (O₂).

flashcards.q: What gas is produced at the positive terminal during water electrolysis?
flashcards.a: Oxygen gas (O₂) is produced at the positive terminal (anode) during water electrolysis.

flashcards.q: What gas is produced at the negative terminal during water electrolysis?
flashcards.a: Hydrogen gas (H₂) is produced at the negative terminal (cathode) during water electrolysis.

flashcards.q: What is the ratio of hydrogen to oxygen produced during water electrolysis?
flashcards.a: The volume ratio is approximately 2:1 (hydrogen : oxygen), confirming the chemical formula H₂O—two hydrogen atoms for every oxygen atom.

flashcards.q: How do you identify hydrogen gas experimentally?
flashcards.a: Bring a burning candle or splinter to the mouth of the test tube containing hydrogen gas. Hydrogen burns with a characteristic 'pop' sound.

flashcards.q: How do you identify oxygen gas experimentally?
flashcards.a: Bring a burning candle to the mouth of the test tube containing oxygen gas. The flame glows brighter, confirming oxygen's combustion-supporting property.

flashcards.q: Why is lime water used to detect carbon dioxide in air?
flashcards.a: Lime water (calcium hydroxide solution) turns milky when exposed to air because carbon dioxide in air reacts with calcium hydroxide to form calcium carbonate (white precipitate): Ca(OH)₂ + CO₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O.

flashcards.q: What is calcium carbonate?
flashcards.a: Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) is a white, insoluble solid formed when carbon dioxide reacts with calcium hydroxide. It is the same compound found in limestone, chalk, and eggshells.

flashcards.q: What observation indicates the presence of carbon dioxide in air?
flashcards.a: Lime water turns milky (opaque, cloudy white appearance), indicating the presence of carbon dioxide.

flashcards.q: How do you prepare lime water?
flashcards.a: Add calcium oxide (quick lime) slowly to water. The exothermic reaction produces calcium hydroxide: CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂ + Heat. Stir and filter to obtain colorless lime water solution.

flashcards.q: What is dust in air?
flashcards.a: Dust particles are suspended solids in air. They are not integral parts of air but are considered pollutants. Their nature and number vary from time to time and from place to place.

flashcards.q: What is the air quality index (AQI)?
flashcards.a: The air quality index is a tool used to describe and measure air quality, accounting for particulate matter (dust, soot) and gaseous pollutants (carbon monoxide, ozone, nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide).

flashcards.q: Name the six types of mixtures based on physical state.
flashcards.a: 1. Gas-gas (air), 2. Gas-liquid (aerated water), 3. Solid-gas (dust in air), 4. Liquid-liquid (vinegar, oil and water), 5. Solid-liquid (seawater, sand and water), 6. Solid-solid (alloys, baking powder).

flashcards.q: Can mixtures be separated by physical methods?
flashcards.a: Yes, mixtures can be separated by physical methods like evaporation, filtration, magnetic separation, and crystallization without breaking chemical bonds.

flashcards.q: Can compounds be separated by physical methods?
flashcards.a: No, compounds cannot be separated into their constituent elements by physical methods. Only chemical methods (like electrolysis or heating) can decompose compounds.

flashcards.q: What is the iron-sulfur mixture experiment?
flashcards.a: Iron filings (black) and sulfur powder (yellow) are mixed to form Sample A (non-uniform mixture). When heated, they chemically combine to form iron sulfide (Sample B), a uniform black compound.

flashcards.q: What is the key difference between Sample A and Sample B in the iron-sulfur experiment?
flashcards.a: Sample A (mixture) shows black and yellow particles separately, is attracted by magnet, and components retain individual properties. Sample B (compound) is uniformly black, not magnetic, and has completely different properties.

flashcards.q: How does the magnet test distinguish between the mixture and compound in the iron-sulfur experiment?
flashcards.a: Sample A (mixture): Iron is attracted to magnet and can be separated. Sample B (compound): Not attracted to magnet because iron has lost its magnetic property when bonded to sulfur in iron sulfide (FeS).

flashcards.q: What happens when Sample A is treated with dilute hydrochloric acid?
flashcards.a: Only iron reacts: Fe + HCl → FeCl₂ + H₂ (hydrogen gas with 'pop' sound). Sulfur does not react and remains as yellow solid.

flashcards.q: What happens when Sample B is treated with dilute hydrochloric acid?
flashcards.a: The entire compound reacts: FeS + HCl → FeCl₂ + H₂S (hydrogen sulfide gas with rotten egg odour).

flashcards.q: Why does the gas smell different in Sample A versus Sample B when treated with acid?
flashcards.a: Sample A produces hydrogen (colorless, no smell, 'pop' sound) because only iron reacts. Sample B produces hydrogen sulfide (rotten egg odour) because the compound (iron sulfide) reacts completely.

flashcards.q: What is sugar decomposition?
flashcards.a: When sugar is heated, it turns brown then black (charred), releasing water vapour. The black residue is carbon. This proves sugar (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) is a compound of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen.

flashcards.q: Why does water appear during sugar decomposition?
flashcards.a: Water comes from the sugar itself, not from air condensation (since the tube is being heated). Water's presence confirms that hydrogen is part of sugar's composition.

flashcards.q: What is the black residue left after heating sugar?
flashcards.a: Charcoal (carbon) is the black residue. It is one of the elements in sugar's composition, proving sugar is a compound.

flashcards.q: Why is sugar a compound and not an element?
flashcards.a: Sugar decomposes when heated into simpler substances (carbon and water). Elements cannot be broken down further. Since sugar can be decomposed, it must be a compound.

flashcards.q: What compounds are formed when iron reacts with moist air?
flashcards.a: Iron reacts with moist air to form iron oxide (rust). The chemical process is: 4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ or similar iron oxide compounds.

flashcards.q: What is magnesium oxide?
flashcards.a: Magnesium oxide (MgO) is a compound formed when magnesium burns in oxygen: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. It is a white solid with completely different properties from magnesium metal and oxygen gas.

flashcards.q: What is a mineral?
flashcards.a: A mineral is a natural, solid substance found on Earth with a fixed chemical composition. Most are compounds, but some are native minerals (pure elements like gold or silver).

flashcards.q: What are native minerals?
flashcards.a: Native minerals are pure elemental forms found naturally in Earth. Examples: Gold (Au), Silver (Ag), Copper (Cu), Sulfur (S), Carbon (diamond).

flashcards.q: What are compound minerals?
flashcards.a: Compound minerals are made of two or more elements in fixed ratios. Examples: Quartz (SiO₂), Calcite (CaCO₃), Mica, Pyroxene, Olivine.

flashcards.q: What is quartz and what are its uses?
flashcards.a: Quartz is a compound mineral with formula SiO₂ (silicon dioxide). It is used in glass production, electronics, and as a building material.

flashcards.q: What is calcite and what are its uses?
flashcards.a: Calcite is a compound mineral with formula CaCO₃ (calcium carbonate). It is used in cement production, steel manufacturing, and chemical industries.

flashcards.q: What is cement made of?
flashcards.a: Cement is made from mineral components: calcite (calcium carbonate), quartz (silicon dioxide), alumina, and iron oxide.

flashcards.q: What is talcum powder made of?
flashcards.a: Talcum powder is made from the mineral talc, which is a magnesium silicate compound.

flashcards.q: What is Dhokra art?
flashcards.a: Dhokra art is an ancient craft from Bihar and Odisha, India, that uses molten brass or bronze (metal alloys) poured into clay moulds to create beautiful, durable figures.

flashcards.q: How is Dhokra art created?
flashcards.a: A design is shaped in beeswax, covered with clay to make a mould. The clay is hardened, wax is melted out leaving a hollow cavity, and molten brass or bronze is poured into the mould.

flashcards.q: Why is Dhokra art valuable?
flashcards.a: The metal alloys (brass, bronze) used in Dhokra art are stronger and more durable than pure metals. The craftsmanship demonstrates understanding of metal properties and traditional artistic techniques.

flashcards.q: What is graphene aerogel?
flashcards.a: Graphene aerogel is a revolutionary material made from carbon. It is the lightest solid material known, so light that grass can hold its weight, yet possesses significant structural integrity.

flashcards.q: What is the primary environmental application of graphene aerogel?
flashcards.a: Graphene aerogel can absorb oil from seawater and land-based spills. Its high porosity and absorption capacity make it effective for cleaning oil spills in oceans and contaminated areas.

flashcards.q: Why is graphene aerogel useful for environmental cleanup?
flashcards.a: Its exceptional absorption capacity, low density, and ability to selectively absorb oil over water make it effective for rapid, efficient cleanup of oil spills without additional pollution.

flashcards.q: What other applications does graphene aerogel have?
flashcards.a: Energy-saving devices, capacitors and battery technologies, thermal insulation for buildings, cryogenic applications, and protective coatings for buildings.

flashcards.q: How does understanding elements and compounds lead to material innovation?
flashcards.a: Knowledge of element properties and bonding patterns enables scientists to design specific atomic arrangements, creating materials with tailored properties for specific applications.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between air composition and life?
flashcards.a: Oxygen in air is essential for respiration in most organisms. Nitrogen does not support combustion but is essential for soil fertility. Carbon dioxide is used by plants in photosynthesis.

flashcards.q: Why can elements exist in different forms (allotropes)?
flashcards.a: Elements can combine in different atomic arrangements, creating different forms with different properties. Example: Carbon exists as diamond (hard), graphite (soft), and graphene (one-layer sheets).

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is formed when two or more substances are mixed without reacting chemically.
flashcards.a: mixture

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ consists of only one type of particle and cannot be separated by physical methods.
flashcards.a: pure substance

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: An _____ is the smallest particle of an element.
flashcards.a: atom

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is a stable particle formed when atoms combine.
flashcards.a: molecule

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is a pure substance made of two or more elements in fixed ratio.
flashcards.a: compound

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: An _____ is a uniform mixture of two or more metals.
flashcards.a: alloy

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is a natural, solid substance with fixed chemical composition.
flashcards.a: mineral

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: _____ is an illegal process of adding cheaper substances to a product.
flashcards.a: Adulteration

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: The _____ is approximately 78% of air composition.
flashcards.a: nitrogen (or N₂)

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: _____ turns lime water milky, proving its presence in air.
flashcards.a: Carbon dioxide (or CO₂)

flashcards.q: True or False: Water is an element.
flashcards.a: False; water is a compound made of hydrogen and oxygen.

flashcards.q: True or False: Salt (sodium chloride) is a mixture.
flashcards.a: False; salt is a pure compound formed by chemical bonding of sodium and chlorine.

flashcards.q: True or False: Air is a pure substance.
flashcards.a: False; air is a uniform mixture of multiple gases.

flashcards.q: True or False: Elements can be broken down into simpler substances.
flashcards.a: False; elements are the simplest substances and cannot be broken down further.

flashcards.q: True or False: Compounds can be separated by physical methods.
flashcards.a: False; compounds can only be separated by chemical methods like electrolysis or heating.

flashcards.q: True or False: A mixture's components retain their individual properties.
flashcards.a: True; in a mixture, each component maintains its own properties.

flashcards.q: True or False: A compound's properties are different from its constituent elements.
flashcards.a: True; compounds have completely new properties different from their constituent elements.

flashcards.q: True or False: Iron filings and sulfur powder form a compound when mixed.
flashcards.a: False; just mixing them forms a non-uniform mixture. They form a compound only when heated together.

flashcards.q: True or False: Iron sulfide (FeS) is attracted to a magnet.
flashcards.a: False; iron sulfide is not magnetic even though it contains iron, because iron's properties change in the compound.

flashcards.q: True or False: Sugar can be decomposed by heating.
flashcards.a: True; heating sugar decomposes it into carbon (charcoal) and water.

flashcards.q: True or False: Alloys can be separated into constituent metals using physical methods.
flashcards.a: False; alloys are atomic-level mixtures requiring chemical methods for separation.

flashcards.q: True or False: Stainless steel is stronger than pure iron.
flashcards.a: True; stainless steel (iron + nickel + chromium + carbon) is stronger and more durable than pure iron.

flashcards.q: True or False: Graphene aerogel is made from carbon.
flashcards.a: True; graphene aerogel is a revolutionary carbon-based material with extraordinary lightness and absorption capacity.

flashcards.q: True or False: Native minerals are pure elements found naturally in Earth.
flashcards.a: True; native minerals like gold, silver, and sulfur are pure elemental forms found in nature.

flashcards.q: True or False: All minerals are compounds.
flashcards.a: False; some minerals are native minerals (pure elements), while most are compounds.

flashcards.q: True or False: Carbon dioxide in air can be detected using lime water.
flashcards.a: True; lime water turns milky when exposed to carbon dioxide.

flashcards.q: What is the key distinguishing property between elements and compounds?
flashcards.a: Elements cannot be broken down into simpler substances, while compounds can be decomposed into their constituent elements through chemical processes.

flashcards.q: Why is understanding mixtures and pure substances important in daily life?
flashcards.a: It helps us identify which substances are pure and useful versus those that need separation, affects food quality (adulteration detection), and enables effective material selection for various applications.

flashcards.q: How do physical and chemical properties help classify matter?
flashcards.a: Physical properties (appearance, solubility, melting point) help identify and distinguish substances. Chemical properties (reactivity, decomposition) help determine composition and reveal whether substances are elements, compounds, or mixtures.
